title: SycoPhantasy: Quantifying Sycophancy and Hallucination in Small Open Weight VLMs for Vision-Language Scoring of Fantasy Characters
abstract: This paper introduces a testbed study using text-to-image fantasy character evaluation to study sycophantic behavior in Vision-Language Models (VLMs). The authors introduce the Bluffing Coefficient (Bc) as a novel metric to quantify the mismatch between a VLM's score and the evidence it cites. They found a strong inverse correlation between model size and sycophancy rate (r = -0.96, p = 0.002), ranging from 22.3% (LFM2-VL, 450M) to 6.0% (LLaVA-1.6, 7B).

methodology.approach: Text-to-image fantasy character evaluation as a proxy for VLM evaluator behavior
methodology.dataset: Fantasy NPC descriptions with rich, structured, and verifiable visual attributes (150-250 words per description)
methodology.metric: Bluffing Coefficient (Bc) computed as Bc = S_norm - R+ + R- (normalized score minus positive evidence recall plus negative recall)
methodology.tools: spaCy for keyphrase extraction, sentence embeddings for matching model reasoning

research_questions: Do small, open-weight VLMs exhibit sycophantic behavior when evaluating image-text alignment?
Is there a relationship between model size and sycophancy?
What patterns emerge when we measure the gap between scores and cited visual evidence?

contributions: The Bluffing Coefficient as a novel metric for quantifying score-evidence mismatch in VLM evaluations
A large-scale benchmark of 173,810 image-description pairs for studying sycophancy in visual scoring
An empirical analysis demonstrating a significant inverse relationship between model size and sycophancy
